{{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site |name = Tsukinooka Kofun |image = [[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]] |image_caption = Mound (right: front part, left: rear circular part) |alternate_name = Tsukinooka Kofun |affiliation = Wakamiya Kofun Group |location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Ukiha|ja|うきは市}}, Yoshii Town, Wakamiya 358-1 (Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine grounds) |latitude = 33.34501 |longitude = 130.76544 |ISO = JP-40 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} |length = 80m (95m including base terrace)
Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鉄鏃 (橿考研保管).JPG|Iron arrowheads
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Tsukinooka Kofun. == See Also == * Wakamiya Kofun Group ''' [[Tsukadō Kofun]] ''' [[Hioka Kofun]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsukinooka Kofun}} * {{National Important Cultural Property Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鉄鏃 (橿考研保管).JPG|Iron arrowheads
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from {{Infobox {{Infobox ancient site |name = Tsukinooka Kofun |image = [[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]] |image_caption = Mound (right: front part, left: rear circular part) |alternate_name = Tsukinooka Kofun |affiliation = Wakamiya Kofun Group |location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Ukiha|ja|うきは市}}, Yoshii Town, Wakamiya 358-1 (Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine grounds) |latitude = 33.34501 |longitude = 130.76544 |ISO = JP-40 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} |length = 80m (95m including base terrace)
Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鉄鏃 (橿考研保管).JPG|Iron arrowheads
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Tsukinooka Kofun. == See Also == * Wakamiya Kofun Group ''' [[Tsukadō Kofun]] ''' [[Hioka Kofun]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsukinooka Kofun}} * {{National Important Cultural Property Database|筑後国浮羽郡月岡古墳出土品}} * [https://www.city.ukiha.fukuoka.jp/kiji0035112/index.html Wakamiya Kofun Group (Hioka, Tsukinooka, Tsukadō)] – Ukiha City Website The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet

[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet

Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==

[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment

{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment

{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment

{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from [[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Tsukinooka Kofun |image = [[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]] |image_caption = Mound (right: front part, left: rear circular part) |alternate_name = Tsukinooka Kofun |affiliation = Wakamiya Kofun Group |location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Ukiha|ja|うきは市}}, Yoshii Town, Wakamiya 358-1 (Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine grounds) |latitude = 33.34501 |longitude = 130.76544 |ISO = JP-40 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} |length = 80m (95m including base terrace)
Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鉄鏃 (橿考研保管).JPG|Iron arrowheads
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Tsukinooka Kofun. == See Also == * Wakamiya Kofun Group ''' [[Tsukadō Kofun]] ''' [[Hioka Kofun]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsukinooka Kofun}} * {{National Important Cultural Property The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. {{Infobox ancient site |name = Tsukinooka Kofun |image = [[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]] |image_caption = Mound (right: front part, left: rear circular part) |alternate_name = Tsukinooka Kofun |affiliation = Wakamiya Kofun Group |location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Ukiha|ja|うきは市}}, Yoshii Town, Wakamiya 358-1 (Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine grounds) |latitude = 33.34501 |longitude = 130.76544 |ISO = JP-40 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} |length = 80m (95m including base terrace)
Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鉄鏃 (橿考研保管).JPG|Iron arrowheads
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Tsukinooka Kofun. == See Also == * Wakamiya Kofun Group ''' [[Tsukadō Kofun]] ''' [[Hioka Kofun]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsukinooka Kofun}} * {{National Important Cultural Property Database|筑後国浮羽郡月岡古墳出土品}} * [https://www.city.ukiha.fukuoka.jp/kiji0035112/index.html Wakamiya Kofun Group (Hioka, Tsukinooka, Tsukadō)] – Ukiha City Website {{Infobox ancient site |name = Tsukinooka Kofun |image = [[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]] |image_caption = Mound (right: front part, left: rear circular part) |alternate_name = Tsukinooka Kofun |affiliation = Wakamiya Kofun Group |location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Ukiha|ja|うきは市}}, Yoshii Town, Wakamiya 358-1 (Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine grounds) |latitude = 33.34501 |longitude = 130.76544 |ISO = JP-40 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} |length = 80m (95m including base terrace)
Height: 7m (rear circular part) |burial_facility = Rear circular part: {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}
(containing {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})
Constriction area: Unknown 1
Front part: Unknown 1 |artifacts = 8 sets of armor, bronze mirrors, ornaments, horse fittings, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |satellite_tombs = |construction_period = Mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |buried_person = (Hypothesized) {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan |imperial_tomb = |historic_sites_status = None |tangible_cultural_properties = Artifacts (National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}) |notable_features = |map = Japan Fukuoka |icon = Zenpokoenfun-W |label = Tsukinooka Kofun |label_position = top }} '''Tsukinooka Kofun''' (月岡古墳, also Tsukinooka Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} located in Yoshii Town, {{ill|Ukiha, Fukuoka|ja|うきは市}}, Fukuoka Prefecture. It is part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group and features a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳}} (keyhole-shaped mound). While not designated as a historic site, its artifacts are recognized as a National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}}. == Overview ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+Wakamiya Kofun Group !Kofun name !colspan=2|Mound Type !Burial Facility !Construction Period !Historic Status !Notes |- |'''Tsukinooka Kofun'''||Zenpokoenfun||95m||{{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}
({{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}})||Mid-5th century||None||Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property |- |{{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||91m||Two horizontal stone chambers||Late 5th century||None|| |- |{{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}||Zenpokoenfun||74m||One horizontal stone chamber||Early 6th century||National Historic Site||Decorated kofun |}
[[File:Tsukinooka Kofun, kouenbu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Rear circular part of the mound}}]] Tsukinooka Kofun is located on a plateau south of the {{ill|Chikugo River|ja|筑後川}} in southern Fukuoka Prefecture. It forms part of the Wakamiya Kofun Group alongside {{ill|Tsukadō Kofun|ja|塚堂古墳}} and {{ill|Hioka Kofun|ja|日岡古墳}}. Currently, the mound lies within the grounds of Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine, with a shrine building (covering the stone sarcophagus) at its peak. The southern side of the mound has been altered for residential use. Excavations of the stone chamber during the late Edo period yielded grave goods, and a formal investigation was conducted in 1985. The mound is oriented along an east-west axis, with the front part facing west. It measures 80 meters in length but extends to 95 meters when including the basal terrace.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The mound features {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, including figurative haniwa (human, house, waterfowl, lid-shaped, and armor-shaped). Surrounding the mound are three concentric moats, the innermost believed to have contained water.{{Sfn|解説シート}} The burial chamber is a {{ill|Tateana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}, housing a {{ill|Nagamochi-style sarcophagus|ja|長持形石棺}} made from Aso welded tuff. The sarcophagus interior is painted with vermilion.{{Sfn|解説シート}} Excavations in the 1980s identified additional burial facilities in the constriction area and front part, though these remain unstudied.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} Construction is dated to the mid-{{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, specifically the mid-5th century. Among the Wakamiya Kofun Group, it predates both Tsukadō Kofun and Hioka Kofun. The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鉄鏃 (橿考研保管).JPG|Iron arrowheads
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Tsukinooka Kofun. == See Also == * Wakamiya Kofun Group ''' [[Tsukadō Kofun]] ''' [[Hioka Kofun]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsukinooka Kofun}} * {{National Important Cultural Property Database|筑後国浮羽郡月岡古墳出土品}} * [https://www.city.ukiha.fukuoka.jp/kiji0035112/index.html Wakamiya Kofun Group (Hioka, Tsukinooka, Tsukadō)] – Ukiha City Website The sarcophagus design, rare in Kyushu, parallels those used for influential individuals in the Kinai region, indicating connections to the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|ヤマト王権}}. Based on the area’s name, "Ukiha," and the large amount of armor interred, it is hypothesized that the buried individual belonged to the {{ill|Matō no omi|ja|的臣}} clan, which was involved in military and diplomatic efforts with the Korean Peninsula.{{Sfn|広報うきは2023}} Artifacts recovered from the site were designated as Important Cultural Property in 1961. The sarcophagus is preserved within the shrine building atop the rear circular mound and is opened for viewing on the third Saturday of each month. == History == * {{ill|Bunka (era)|ja|文化}} 2 (1805): Discovery and excavation by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine priest Anyamoto Ōi. Artifacts were recorded in the "Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects," now preserved at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.{{Sfn|若宮古墳群（平凡社）|2004}} * {{ill|Kaei|ja|嘉永}} 6 (1853): Description of artifacts in Yanō Kazutada's "Chikugo Shōshi Gundan."{{Sfn|月岡古墳（古墳）|1989}} * 1925: First formally reported in Volume 1 of the "Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments Survey Report." * 1961: Artifacts designated as Important Cultural Property by the Japanese government.{{Sfn|国指定}} * 1985: Excavation conducted, confirming triple moats. Findings were reported in 1989 by the Yoshii Town Board of Education. == Artifacts == === Important Cultural Property (Nationally Designated) === [[File:月岡宮掘開記.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Tsukinooka Inventory of Ancient Objects}}{{small|Displayed at the Yoshii History and Folklore Museum.}}]] * Artifacts excavated from Tsukinooka Kofun (Chikugo Province, Ukiha District) – Archaeological materials preserved by Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine and Yoshii History and Folklore Museum. Designated on February 17, 1961.{{Sfn|国指定}} ''' Gold-bronze helmet with eyebrow ornament (1) ''' Gold-bronze shin guards (1 set) ''' Gold-bronze belt fittings (set) ''' Gold-bronze quiver fragments (1 set) ''' Bronze mirrors (4) ''' Haniwa house fragments (1) ''' Accompanying excavation documents (1 volume) 月岡古墳 金銅装小札鋲留眉庇付冑 (橿考研保管).JPG|Gold-bronze helmet
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鋲留眉庇付冑片 (橿考研保管).JPG|Helmet fragment
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} 月岡古墳 鉄鏃 (橿考研保管).JPG|Iron arrowheads
{{small|Preserved at the Nara Prefectural Kashihara Archaeological Institute.}} == Related Facilities == * Yoshii History and Folklore Museum (Ukiha, Fukuoka) – Preserves and displays artifacts from Tsukinooka Kofun. == See Also == * Wakamiya Kofun Group ''' [[Tsukadō Kofun]] ''' [[Hioka Kofun]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tsukinooka Kofun}} * {{National Important Cultural Property
